Article Title: The CEO’s AI quotient: Why the intelligence of your business starts in the boardroom
Article Text:
Source: Gartner CxO Survey, December 2025, cited in Gartner Newsroom, May 2026

What High-AIQ Leadership Looks Like



Source:

McKinsey

State of AI, 2025

Source: Gartner, ‘Predicts by 2027: 50% of Enterprises Without a People-Centric AI Strategy Will Lose Their Top AI Talent’, May 2026

The Pilot Project Trap



Source: Gartner, ‘Predicts 30% of

Generative AI Projects Will Be Abandoned

After POC By End of 2025’, July 2024

Source: McKinsey State of AI, 2025; sranalytics.io analysis of McKinsey data, November 2025

The ROI Evidence Is Clear — But Only for Those Who Approach It Strategically

Source: Google Cloud AI ROI Study, September 2025, cited in PUNKU.AI, November 2025; McKinsey State of AI, 2025

India’s Leadership Imperative

Source: Deloitte State of AI in the Enterprise, India Report, March 2026

AI does not transform businesses: Leaders do. The companies achieving the highest returns from AI are not those with the most advanced technology but those whose leadership has made the deepest strategic commitment to it. This is the AI Quotient of leadership, and it is the single most important variable in determining whether AI investment creates value or disappears into a pilot project graveyard.In December 2025, Gartner surveyed 197 CxOs and senior business leaders on their AI preparedness. Only 27 per cent of executives had a comprehensive AI strategy with only 20 per cent believed their workforce was truly AI-ready. And despite this, 78 per cent of their organisations reported using AI in at least one business function.This is the central paradox of AI adoption in 2026: the technology is everywhere, but the strategic leadership to harness it is not, and also there is a shortage of AI talent. Companies are deploying AI tools with a limited AI strategy. They are measuring usage rather than impact and investing in systems without investing in the culture required to use them well.The gap between AI adoption and AI Quotient is, in most cases, a leadership gap.Recently, several tech giants have restricted the use of AI tools ‘for the sake of using them’. This suggests that employees were told to use AI at work to improve efficiency but the usage did not bring out any significant changes.The McKinsey State of AI 2025 report identified a clear pattern among organisations achieving the highest returns from AI. Their leaders connect every AI initiative to a financial model early. They track not just adoption metrics: active users, tasks automated but quality metrics and business results: EBIT impact, revenue lift, customer satisfaction improvement. They treat data infrastructure as a strategic investment, not an IT cost centre. And they make AI accountability a board-level agenda item, not a committee recommendation.Gartner’s research quantifies what this leadership approach produces. Employees who are proficient with AI across multiple use cases are 2x as likely to be highly productive, 2.3x more likely to deliver high-quality work, and 3.2x more likely to drive effective process improvements. These numbers are not generated by deploying a better algorithm. They are generated by building a better culture — which is a leadership function.Gartner predicted in 2024 that 30 per cent of generative AI projects would be abandoned after proof of concept by the end of 2025, with poor data quality, escalating costs, and unclear business value identified as the primary causes. The data from 2025 confirmed the prediction — and in many organisations, the number was higher.The common thread in failed AI pilots is not technical failure. It is strategic failure. A pilot project without a defined path to scale is not a learning exercise — it is an expensive way to generate a press release. High-AIQ leaders understand this. They design AI initiatives with scaling criteria built in from the beginning: what metrics must be achieved, at what cost, over what timeline, to justify enterprise-wide deployment?McKinsey’s analysis found that scaling is the bottleneck in most AI programmes. Many firms report pilots; few show end-to-end transformation or EBIT impact at enterprise level. The McKinsey data showed value concentration in four functions: customer operations (82 per cent faster response times), marketing and sales (85 per cent higher click-through rates), software engineering (45 per cent productivity gains), and R&D. High-AIQ leaders direct AI investment toward these high-value applications rather than distributing it across a portfolio of experiments that collectively demonstrate nothing.Google Cloud’s September 2025 study of 3,466 senior leaders across 24 countries found that 74 per cent achieved ROI within the first year of AI deployment, and 56 per cent reported revenue gains, with most estimating 6 to 10 per cent increases. AI investments are generating returns. But the distribution of those returns is extremely unequal. Only 6 per cent of organisations qualify as ‘AI high performers’ with more than 5 per cent EBIT impact. The other 94 per cent are generating sub-threshold returns from their AI investments.The differentiating factor, consistently, is strategic clarity. Gartner’s findings indicate that organisations with a clear AI strategy are measurably more likely to achieve positive ROI. The strategy precedes the technology. The vision precedes the vendor selection. The business case precedes the budget approval.India’s enterprise AI story in 2026 is one of impressive breadth and uneven depth. The Deloitte India report of March 2026 confirmed that Indian enterprises lead global peers in at-scale AI deployment across product development, strategy, operations, and marketing. But the same report noted that the transition from at-scale to enterprise-transformative is the challenge that remains. This transition is, fundamentally, a leadership challenge.The TOI AI Quotient Awards, in its leadership categories, seeks to identify the Indian business leaders who have made this transition possible in their organisations. Not the leaders with the most ambitious AI vision — but those who have translated vision into measurable, documented, enterprise-wide business value.
Article Summary: Gartner surveyed 197 CxOs and senior business leaders on their AI preparedness. Only 27 per cent of executives had a comprehensive AI strategy with only 20 per cent believed their workforce was truly AI-ready. The technology is everywhere, but the strategic leadership to harness it is not. There is a shortage of AI talent.

Article Title: Katie Miller is not impressed with Jeff Bezos' ex-wife MacKenzie Scott who has donated billions; says: MacKenzie Scott has given away $26 billion, almost entirely to groups that seek to dismantle …
Article Text:
“MacKenzie Scott has given away $26 billion — almost entirely to progressive groups that seek to dismantle the very way of life that built her fortune:

* $275M to Planned Parenthood

* $51M to The Trevor Project for LGBTQ youth

* $1.2B for racial equity education

* $586M to racial justice groups—including Movement for Black Lives

* $163M to LGBTQ organizations including Transgender Law Center

* $133M to gender equity

* $125M to climate justice

* $200M to open borders immigration groups"

"Sadly yes"

"spending it making the world a worse place."

MacKenzie Scott has donated over $26 billion for philanthropy



"Super rich ex-wives who hate their former spouse should be listed among 'Reasons that Western Civilization died,”

Amazon founder Jeff Bezos' ex-wife, MacKenzie Scott, has failed to impress podcaster and former adviser to the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), Katie Miller. She criticised Scott's charitable giving in a post on X, claiming that billions of dollars donated by the billionaire author and philanthropist have largely gone to organisations promoting progressive causes.In her post, Miller wrote:Miller's remarks come days after Elon Musk also criticised Scott's philanthropy, respondingto a post claiming that the billionaire wasScott has donated more than $26 billion through her philanthropic organisation, Yield Giving, since 2020. Her donations have supported thousands of non-profit organisations working in areas including education, housing, healthcare, racial equity, climate initiatives and community development.Earlier this year, Scott donated $70 million to Meals on Wheels America to help expand meal delivery and care services for seniors and homebound individuals across the US. The organisation said the unrestricted funding would strengthen its nationwide network of more than 5,000 community-based programmes.Scott has also donated $7 million to Red Lake Nation College and $42 million to Elizabeth City State University, taking her total contributions to historically Black colleges and universities beyond $1 billion.Other recipients include Howard University, Spelman College, Habitat for Humanity, climate initiatives and Girl Scouts of the USA.Scott received a 4% stake in Amazon as part of her 2019 divorce settlement with Jeff Bezos and has pledged to donate the majority of her wealth. This is not the first time Scott's charitable giving has drawn criticism from Elon Musk. In 2024, he posted a message on X that many interpreted as referring to Scott, writing:Musk said,Although Musk did not mention Scott by name, the post came amid discussions surrounding her donations to organisations focused on race, gender, climate and other social causes. The post was later deleted.According to America's Top 25 Philanthropists list, Scott ranks third with lifetime donations of $26.4 billion, behind Warren Buffett and Bill and Melinda Gates.
Article Summary: MacKenzie Scott has donated over $26 billion for philanthropy. Her donations have supported thousands of non-profit organisations working in areas including education, housing, healthcare, racial equity, climate initiatives and community development. Scott received a 4% stake in Amazon as part of her divorce settlement with Jeff Bezos and has pledged to donate the majority of her wealth.

Article Title: Aarogya Setu 2.0 launches with Google's Gemma 4 and open Medical Data Toolkit
Article Text:
Ms Preeti Lobana, Vice President and Country Manager, Google India with Dr. Sunil Kumar Barnwal (IAS), Chief Executive Officer, National Health Authority at the launch of the Aarogya Setu 2.0 App at Vigyan Bhawan

India's National Health Authority launched the new Aarogya Setu 2.0 app on Monday, rebuilding the COVID-era contact-tracing tool into a unified platform for personal health records and government health services. The app runs on Google's Gemma 4 open models and its Medical Data Toolkit, which together turn messy medical paperwork into something patients and doctors can actually use.The problem they're solving is a familiar one. Health records arrive as a jumble of lab reports, scans, and printouts, often unstructured and hard to read. Gemma 4 and the toolkit work in tandem to identify what kind of record they're looking at, then pull out the details that matter, like test names, methods, and results, from both text and images.The toolkit then maps that information into FHIR data, the standard format that lets different healthcare systems share records without friction. The practical payoff: when a patient moves between hospitals or providers, their history travels with them instead of getting lost in translation. Inside the app, users stay in control of all their health records.Google has also open-sourced the Medical Data Toolkit, handing it to developers and health organisations at no cost.The first release is tuned for the most common record types, such as lab reports and observations, and helps apps comply with interoperability standards under the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission. Gemma 4 itself launched back in April 2026.Preeti Lobana, Vice President and Country Manager at Google India , said the tools are meant to give Indians tighter control over their health journeys while lowering the barrier for health-tech startups. The toolkit builds on an earlier Google release, a wrapper that helped Indian developers plug their apps into the ABDM architecture.
Article Summary: India's National Health Authority launched the new Aarogya Setu 2.0 app on Monday. The app runs on Google's Gemma 4 open models and its Medical Data Toolkit, which together turn messy medical paperwork into something patients and doctors can actually use.

Article Title: Nvidia to offer affordable AI computing to Australia's Firmus Technologies under new deal
Article Text:
What Firmus co-CEO Tim Rosenfield said about the Nvidia deal



Nvidia has signed a strategic partnership with Australian AI infrastructure company Firmus Technologies. According to a Reuters report, the US chip giant will help provide emerging artificial intelligence (AI) companies with more affordable access to computing power under this deal. Under the agreement, Firmus will purchase Nvidia infrastructure and offer Nvidia-powered cloud services to customers, including AI-native companies.The partnership will allow Nvidia to earn revenue from hardware sales and a share of cloud service revenue generated through the collaboration. Firmus said the arrangement aims to make AI infrastructure more accessible to smaller companies developing AI apps. As part of the agreement, Firmus will deploy 170,000 Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) between the first quarter of 2027 and early 2028. The GPU infrastructure will be located in Batam, Indonesia, according to the Reuters report.The Australian company said it expects to generate up to $30 billion in revenue during the first six years of the agreement, based on customer commitments.Explaining the objective of the partnership, Tim Rosenfield, co-chief executive of Firmus Technologies, told Reuters, “We have worked to figure out how to close the gap ‌between the cost benefits that the large guys have access to, which they do because they have great credit ratings, and the guys that are up-and-comers.This is actually a really material way to level the playing field a little bit to give the next a chance to compete with the big guys. ”Nvidia is already an investor in Firmus Technologies, having participated in the company's previous fundraising rounds. In April, Firmus said it had raised $1.35 billion over the previous six months, giving it a post-money valuation of $5.5 billion. According to people familiar with the matter, the company has also appointed investment banks to work on a potential initial public offering (IPO). However, Rosenfield declined to comment on the company's IPO plans.The partnership comes as demand for AI infrastructure continues to increase, with companies seeking access to high-performance GPUs to build and deploy AI models.
Article Summary: N Nvidia has signed a strategic partnership with Australian AI infrastructure company Firmus Technologies. Firmus will purchase Nvidia infrastructure and offer Nvidia-powered cloud services to customers. The partnership will allow Nvidia to earn revenue from hardware sales and a share of cloud service revenue generated through the collaboration.

Article Title: As Meta keeps delaying the release of its new AI model to developers, Google's message to Meta comes in picture
Article Text:
Meta forced into delays and token rations



Silicon Valley’s ongoing race for artificial intelligence (AI) dominance has hit a massive bottleneck: Computing power. Even tech giants like Google and Facebook-parent company Meta are also feeling its heat. A report has claimed that Google has officially placed strict limits on Meta’s access to its premier Gemini AI models after Mark Zuckerberg’s social media giant tried to buy up more computing capacity than Google could physically provide.Citing people familiar with the matter, The Financial Times reports that Google informed Meta that it does not have the server space or computing capacity to fulfill Meta’s massive orders. The squeeze is a direct result of the explosive global demand for “inference workloads” – the computing power required to actually enable AI chatbots, coding assistants and automated agents to quickly respond to tasks assigned to them.Even Google's multi-billion-dollar network of data centers is buckling under the weight of the AI boom.“Obviously, we are compute-constrained in the near term. Our Cloud revenue would have been higher if we were able to meet the demand," Google CEO Sundar Pichai acknowledged at the company’s first-quarter earnings in April, noting that Google Cloud's backlog of signed-but-undelivered contracts recently doubled to a staggering $460 billionTo survive the hardware drought, Google recently signed a massive $920 million-a-month deal to lease extra computing capacity from Elon Musk’s SpaceX network.Rival AI lab Anthropic reportedly signed a similar emergency capacity deal with SpaceX last month.Google’s computing cap may have already disrupted and delayed Meta’s highly anticipated plans to release its newest AI technology to outside developers. Unlike Google or Microsoft, Meta does not own a commercial cloud computing business. While Zuckerberg has committed to spending a $600 billion on US data centers by 2028 to build what he calls a “personal superintelligence,” the company currently remains dependent on its rivals.Meta had been quietly using Google’s Gemini to run its own internal operations, which include automated customer service chatbots and AI systems that root out scams and harmful content, because Gemini outperformed Meta's own open-source “Llama” models, the report said.Because of Google’s capacity limits, Meta has been forced to ration its usage, strictly ordering its engineers to be hyper-efficient with “tokens” (the digital units used to measure AI processing). Earlier this month, Meta even delayed plans to release its newest AI model to developers.
Article Summary: Google reportedly told Facebook-parent company Meta that it does not have the server space or computing capacity to fulfill Meta’s massive orders. The squeeze is a direct result of the explosive global demand for “inference workloads” – the computing power required to enable AI chatbots, coding assistants and automated agents.

Article Title: Lawsuit filed against Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron, claims these companies restricted RAM supply to inflate prices
Article Text:
Memory giants sued again as RAM prices hit record highs

What the lawsuit actually accuses the three of doing

Why no rival can step in to undercut them

This isn't the first time these names have come up

The three companies that make nearly all the world's memory chips are now facing a class action lawsuit in the US, accused of quietly choking off DRAM supply to drive prices through the roof. Samsung , SK Hynix and Micron—the trio behind almost the entire global DRAM market — stand accused of coordinating production cuts while memory costs climbed at a pace the lawsuit describes as having "mind-blowing scale and rapidity."Filed on June 25 in the US District Court for the Northern District of California under case number 3:26-cv-06345, the complaint lands at a moment when RAM prices have hit historic highs and consumers are already feeling the squeeze on everything from laptops to game consoles.The complaint alleges that the three firms worked together to fix prices while reducing the supply of conventional memory. Specifically, it claims they cut back on DDR3 and DDR4 RAM and pushed most of their production toward HBM—a pricier, high-bandwidth form of 3D-stacked DRAM that AI data centres are hungry for. The result, the suit argues, is that ordinary buyers paid inflated prices for a market deliberately "crippled by the behavior of DRAM oligopolists.There's an economic logic the plaintiffs lean on, too. In a healthy market, soaring prices should pull more supply toward them—at least one company should have ramped up output to grab customers. The suit says that simply never happened. Instead, the trio kept squeezing supply, in some cases junking conventional DRAM channels altogether while publicly steering resources to HBM.Part of what makes the allegation stick, according to the filing, is that nobody else can realistically challenge these three. Building a single DRAM fabrication plant can cost tens of billions of dollars and take years to finish. Even then, the manufacturing know-how represents "decades of accumulated trade secrets," and US export controls keep Chinese rivals from getting current-generation equipment. The practical upshot, the suit claims, is that when the big three restrict supply, no outsider can expand output to bring prices down.The case is being heard by Judge Noel Wise, with attorneys from Bathaee Dunne LLP representing a group of plaintiffs that includes individual consumers and small PC repair and fabrication businesses.The lawsuit points out that this is familiar territory. Back in 2005, Samsung pleaded guilty and paid a $300 million fine over what the US Department of Justice called an international conspiracy to fix DRAM prices. Hynix pleaded guilty around the same time and was fined $185 million, while Micron reportedly dodged a penalty by reporting the conduct and cooperating with prosecutors. China also investigated the three when prices spiked between 2016 and 2018. As the complaint puts it, this is the third such cycle in the same market among the same firms.The timing stings for consumers. Apple raised prices across nearly its whole hardware lineup the same day, hiking MacBooks and iPads by up to 20%. And it didn’t just hit customer, the new triggered a 6% stock drop—its worst single day in over a year. Microsoft has also raised Xbox prices by $100 to $150, citing memory and storage costs that have "increased by more than 2. 5x." Valve, Sony and Nintendo have all bumped hardware costs too, and analysts warn more increases could follow if the shortage drags on—which, by every available signal, it will.
Article Summary: Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron accused of choking off DRAM supply to drive prices through the roof. Class-action lawsuit filed in US District Court for the Northern District of California. Suit says ordinary buyers paid inflated prices for a market deliberately "crippled by the behavior of DRAM oligopolists"

Article Title: In 2016, Google rebuilt Translate with neural AI, making translations feel more natural
Article Text:
Google Translate connects languages across the world daily. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Why the shift of 2016 was important

From phrase-based to neural

Artificial neural network image recognition. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Improved fluency, but constraints were evident

A turning point for regular users

In 2016, Google quietly shifted its translation app from rule-based machine translation to neural machine translation. Although this was not a breakthrough technology that solved all the existing problems with translations and made them perfect, it greatly improved the fluency of resulting sentences.Language applications are not evaluated only on accuracy; the ability of programs to produce language-like translations is very important too. In 2016, the change reflected the industry trend of moving away from outdated phrase-based systems toward models that are better at tracking grammar and context within larger texts. This was the time when online translation started to become more natural, although many difficult challenges remained.The shift was important because neural machine translation generates sentences differently from earlier approaches. Older translation engines assembled sentences from shorter pieces of language and could sometimes produce unnatural sentences. Neural machine translation models improved sentence fluency by using a larger context window and relying on it more heavily.This change made the results of translations more fluent, as the system generated phrases and sentences more naturally.This did not make the system understand language like humans do, but it did allow for more fluent and natural results. For many people using this tool, it was the first time machine translation felt much more natural and human-like.The problem of getting contextually correct machine translation is still a problem today, according to a review in iScience , but neural methods have enabled completely new ways of research in this area. In particular, the implementation of neural machine translation in 2016 was one of the first signs that this technology began to advance beyond phrase-based machine translation models. Google implemented it into its well-known product.The previous approach to machine translation was heavily based on rules and phrases. This configuration meant that the translation was very efficient in translating small chunks of text, but poor in representing the sentence structure. However, neural machine translation learned to extract patterns from large datasets and use them to generate output more holistically.The effect achieved in this case was not only the improvement in the selection of words, but also in sentence generation. Neural machine translation enhances the quality of translation through improved management of grammar, long-range dependency, and context. These are the exact features that the previous systems struggled with.This upgrade was necessary because translation is judged in practice, not just in theory. A machine can generate perfect word-level translations and still fail to produce a coherent sentence. Neural machine translation closed this gap through enhancing the readability and reliability of the output generated. Thus, the upgrade made the technology more useful for travellers, students, and other casual users who wanted the basic idea of a message.Despite this progress on the technical front, the system had obvious constraints. According to the academic review in the journal iScience, machine translation systems have trouble processing idiomatic phrases, terminology, and context-dependent meanings. This is relevant to the fact that fluency might mask errors – sentences would sound well-formed and yet fail to capture the true meaning.Thus, the 2016 rollout should be seen as a fluency improvement rather than a complete solution. Indeed, the machine learned to mimic natural speech patterns. However, it lacked an understanding of the social or situational context that people take for granted without giving it much thought.In a 2024 article available in PubMed , it is pointed out that neural MT has grown to support highly multilingual capabilities and even zero-shot translation, i.e., the process of translation between language pairs without explicit bilingual data. Within this historical narrative, the 2016 revision affected the public expectation in the way that users did not have to have perfect results to see the difference in quality; they saw that it worked to give a readable line of text.For an average user, the key impact of switching to a neural engine became apparent in one thing only: translations sounded more natural. It was especially useful in messages, on web pages, in menus, and for short travel phrases, where awkward wording can make meaning hard to understand.This is a complete sentence, but the following sentence is missing a clear subject-verb connection and reads awkwardly in context. As a widely used consumer product becomes more human-like, the public expectation of AI capabilities rises accordingly. Improved fluency helped prepare users to accept neural language models, even before they understood the term. The 2016 update became an integral element of the public history of AI.The update fits into a broader shift from manually coded technology toward pattern learning. In the process, it becomes clear that natural language processing is not just coding; it also relies on context, ambiguity, and real-world meaning. Today, users expect translations to be fluent, a standard shaped by the neural shift introduced in 2016.
Article Summary: In 2016, Google quietly shifted its translation app from rule-based machine translation to neural machine translation. This change made the results of translations more fluent, as the system generated phrases and sentences more naturally. For many people using this tool, it was the first time machine translation felt much more natural.

Article Title: Sold a company at 16, raised $3 million at 19, received US O-1 visa: How Dhravya Shah built AI startup Supermemory
Article Text:
From dorm room projects to Supermemory



Dhravya Shah secured $3 million seed funding



Walking away from IIT



Recognition and U.S. O-1 visa



Why Supermemory matters



Mumbai-born entrepreneur Dhravya Shah built his own-company Supermemory, an AI startup that raised $3 million in seed funding. Shah also managed to secure a US O-1 visa. Shah’s entrepreneurial journey started from selling his first company at 16 to walking away from the IIT dream. His journey also highlights how a solo founder turned a dorm room project into a critical infrastructure for AI products. Supermemory began as a hackathon project in 2024 — a consumer “second brain” app that rocketed up the GitHub charts, surpassing 10k stars and becoming one of the fastest-growing OSS projects of the year.“Not long after launching the consumer version of Supermemory, Dhravya started getting inbound from other startups asking for help setting up memory systems for their own products. When Dhravya started pulling the thread, he saw that if he really wanted to have an impact improving memory for AI he would need to start serving other developers. He’s been pursuing it ever since,” reads Solofounders blog.Before starting Supermemory, Shah shipped more than 15 open-source tools from his college dorm, never charging users. One of these a personal context collector gained traction and evolved into Supermemory.His approach has been marked by constant pivots, moving from a consumer “second brain” to the universal memory API that now powers AI applications.In October 2025, Shah raised $3 million (TechCrunch confirmed $2.6M, while Indian outlets reported $3M) in seed funding for Supermemory. The round was led by Susa Ventures, Browder Capital, and SF1.vc, with backing from industry leaders including Google AI chief Jeff Dean, Cloudflare CTO Dane Knecht, DeepMind PM Logan Kilpatrick, and Sentry founder David Cramer. Supermemory’s technology extracts structured “memories” from unstructured data—files, chats, emails—and builds a knowledge graph to surface context for LLM-based apps. Early customers included Cluely, Montra, and SciraShah’s path was unconventional. He abandoned his IIT aspirations to pursue entrepreneurship, a decision that led him to build Supermemory at 19. His story has been widely covered in Indian media as an example of how young founders are reshaping the AI landscape.Shah’s achievements earned him the U.S. O-1 visa, reserved for individuals with extraordinary ability. This recognition underscores his growing influence in the AI ecosystem and validates Supermemory’s role as a foundational layer for AI products.Persistent memory remains a major challenge for AI systems, which often lose context across sessions. Supermemory addresses this gap by enabling multi-session coherence and faster retrieval than retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) alternatives. Its differentiation lies in lower latency and robust access-control design, making it a promising infrastructure tool for developers.
Article Summary: Dhravya Shah built Supermemory, an AI startup that raised $3 million in seed funding. Shah also managed to secure a US O-1 visa. Shah’s entrepreneurial journey started from selling his first company at 16 to walking away from the IIT dream.

Article Title: The iPhone turns 19: Still the gadget that changed modern life
Article Text:
The device that swallowed everything



None of that mattered

The casualties came quickly

One device became dozens

That single shift fundamentally altered consumer electronics

The world's most influential computer



On the evening of June 29, 2007, people queued outside Apple Stores across the United States to buy a phone few had touched but many believed would change everything. There were no livestreamed launch events, countdown timers or influencers posting first impressions. Apple simply announced that the iPhone would go on sale at 6 pm, stores would stay open until midnight, and customers could buy two each on a first-come, first-served basis.Nineteen years later, that understated debut looks almost surreal. The device that quietly went on sale that Friday evening would go on to become the most influential consumer product of the 21st century. It didn't just redefine the mobile phone. It put a capable camera in every pocket, made dedicated music players obsolete, accelerated the rise of social media, transformed banking and commerce, and turned the smartphone into the world's primary computer.The iPhone didn't merely create a new category. It rewrote how billions of people work, communicate, travel, shop and capture their lives.The original iPhone was hardly perfect. It lacked an App Store.It did not support copy and paste. There was no 3G. Its camera would look laughably basic today.What Apple had really built was a computer that happened to make phone calls. More importantly, it convinced people that software—not hardware—would define the future of personal technology.The MP3 player was the first to disappear. Dedicated digital cameras followed. Why carry another device when the camera on your phone kept getting better every year?Then came portable GPS units, pocket dictionaries, alarm clocks, calculators, flashlights, voice recorders and scanners. Boarding passes moved onto the iPhone. Tickets followed. Wallets began shrinking. Bank branches became less relevant. The morning newspaper gave way to notifications.The iPhone didn't invent every category it consumed. It simply made carrying separate devices feel unnecessary.Photography became democratic because everyone suddenly had a capable camera with them all the time. Social media exploded because the camera was connected to the internet. The creator economy owes as much to smartphone cameras as it does to platforms like YouTube, Instagram and TikTok.Entire professions — from ride-hailing drivers to food delivery partners, influencers, livestreamers and mobile-first entrepreneurs —depend on a smartphone that descends directly from that first iPhone.It is difficult to find another consumer product that has quietly rewritten so many industries without physically changing all that much.Today, the iPhone is often judged by incremental upgrades — a better camera, a faster chip, thinner bezels or new AI features.That perspective misses the bigger story.The iPhone's greatest innovation was not Face ID, Retina displays or even multitouch. It was changing humanity's relationship with computing.Before 2007, computers were destinations. You sat down in front of one.The iPhone made computing ambient. It became something that travelled with you, responding instantly whenever you needed it.That change transformed expectations across the technology industry. Every service suddenly had to become mobile-first. Banks built apps instead of branches. Retail shifted to shopping from the sofa. Food delivery became normal. Video calls became effortless. Work, entertainment, payments, navigation and communication converged into a single slab of glass.Even artificial intelligence's current boom is unfolding largely through smartphones. Whether it is voice assistants, visual search, live translation or on-device AI, the smartphone remains the primary gateway through which billions of people experience new technology.Apple has sold billions of iPhones since that first weekend in June 2007. The device has generated trillions of dollars in revenue, transformed Apple's fortunes and inspired an industry that now ships well over a billion smartphones annually.Yet the significance of that first launch cannot be measured only in units sold.It lies in the countless moments it changed everyday life.The parent capturing a child's first steps without planning ahead. The traveller navigating a foreign city without unfolding a paper map. The student carrying an entire library in a pocket. The small business owner accepting digital payments from a roadside stall. The citizen livestreaming history as it unfolds.Those moments became so ordinary that they stopped feeling remarkable.Perhaps that is the iPhone's greatest achievement. It made revolutionary technology feel routine.Back in June 2007, Apple's press release promised free workshops, Genius Bar appointments and late-night store openings. It said almost nothing about changing the world.History filled in the rest.
Article Summary: The first iPhone went on sale on June 29, 2007. It was a computer that happened to make phone calls. Apple has sold billions of iPhones since that first weekend in June 2007. The device has generated trillions of dollars in revenue, transformed Apple's fortunes and inspired an industry that now ships over a billion smartphones annually.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Tim Cook: "Life is fragile. We're not guaranteed a tomorrow so…" - a reminder to live fully now
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Apple CEO Tim Cook (Image source: Wikipedia)

Quote of the day by Tim Cook

"Life is fragile. We're not guaranteed a tomorrow so give it everything you've got."

Who is Tim Cook

The wake-up call behind the quote by Tim Cook



What is the meaning of the quote by Tim Cook

Why this Tim Cook’s quote is relevant

How to apply this quote by Tim Cook in daily life

Other famous quotes by Tim Cook

"Let your joy be in your journey, not in some distant goal."

"For the most important decisions in your life, trust your intuition, and then work with everything you have to prove it right."

"The sidelines are not where you want to live your life. The world needs you in the arena."

"We want to leave the world better than we found it."

Most of us live as though time is unlimited. We put off the call, the trip, the apology, the dream, telling ourselves there's always next year. Tim Cook, the head of Apple , offers a sharper way to look at it. Life is fragile, he said. We're not guaranteed a tomorrow so give it everything you've got. It's a plain, almost blunt reminder, and that's exactly what gives it force. None of us truly knows how much time we have. We act as if tomorrow is promised, yet it never really is. Cook isn't trying to frighten anyone. He's trying to wake us up. If today might be all you can count on, then the only sensible response is to stop holding back, stop waiting for the perfect moment, and pour yourself fully into the life you have right now.Tim Cook is the chief executive of Apple, one of the largest and most valuable companies in the world. He took over in 2011 from Steve Jobs, the company's legendary co-founder, at a moment when many doubted anyone could fill those shoes.Born in Alabama in 1960, Cook joined Apple in 1998 and ran its operations for years before stepping into the top job. Under his leadership, the company grew into a giant worth trillions.He's known less for showmanship than for being calm, disciplined and deeply principled, the steady hand rather than the dazzling visionary. Which makes his reflections on life itself, rather than on technology, all the more worth hearing.Cook has returned to this idea more than once, including in speeches to graduating students just setting out in the world. The thought didn't come from nowhere. He has spoken about a frightening health scare earlier in his life, a moment that forced him to confront how quickly everything you take for granted can change.That kind of experience tends to rearrange your priorities in a hurry. When you've felt how thin the line really is, the small worries shrink and the big questions grow louder. Are you spending your days on what actually matters? Are you holding back out of fear? Cook came away convinced that life is too fragile, and far too short, to coast through on autopilot. The quote is the distilled version of that lesson, handed to the rest of us before we have to learn it the hard way ourselves.The quote has two halves that work together. The first is a hard fact. Life is fragile, and tomorrow isn't guaranteed to anyone, no matter how young, healthy or careful they happen to be. The second half is what we're meant to do about it. Give it everything you've got.Put together, the message is a call to live without holding back. If our time is genuinely uncertain, then half-hearted living becomes a kind of waste. Why save your best effort, your honesty, your love, for some later day that may never actually arrive? Cook is arguing for full commitment to the present, not because life is grim, but precisely because it's so precious. The fragility isn't a reason to despair. It's a reason to show up completely while you can.It's easy to drift through life on autopilot, quietly assuming there's always more time to start really living. We delay joy, postpone risks, and keep our fullest selves in reserve for later. This quote cuts straight through that habit.It doesn't ask for anything dramatic. It simply asks you to stop treating tomorrow as a certainty and to bring more of yourself to today. In a culture that constantly nudges us to wait, to play it safe, to defer happiness until some future milestone is reached, Cook's reminder lands hard. The time to give your life everything you've got isn't someday. It's now, because now is the only part any of us is actually promised.You can take this to heart without overhauling your whole life overnight.Stop postponing what matters. The call you keep meaning to make, the thing you keep meaning to say, the plan you keep delaying, pick one and do it this week.Give your full effort. Whatever today holds, your work, your relationships, your time with the people you love, put your whole self into it rather than half.Take the worthwhile risk. If fear is the only thing standing between you and something meaningful, treat that as a reason to move, not a reason to wait.Appreciate the ordinary day. You don't need a crisis to value your time. A little gratitude for a normal, healthy day is its own quiet way of giving life everything you've got.It's a striking message from a man who runs one of the busiest companies on earth. With everything competing for his attention, Cook's advice comes down to something simple and human. Don't wait. Don't hold back. The one day you can be sure of is this one, so give it all you have. The rest of life, fragile as it is, tends to reward the people who do.
Article Summary: Tim Cook is the chief executive of Apple, one of the largest and most valuable companies in the world. He took over in 2011 from Steve Jobs, the company's legendary co-founder, at a moment when many doubted anyone could fill those shoes. Cook's quote is a call to live without holding back.

Article Title: Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta will soon use AI to do what thousands of human moderators do
Article Text:
Massive shift to automated review



Internal friction and the ‘shadow-ban’ problem



Meta is reportedly accelerating plans to replace thousands of human content moderators with generative artificial intelligence (AI). The transition comes as part of a broader, company-wide cost-cutting drive as the Mark Zuckerberg-led company is spending billions of dollars on advanced AI infrastructure. Citing sources familiar with the matter, The Financial Times reported that the tech group has fast-tracked the deployment of large language models (LLMs) to review posts and advertisements across Facebook and Instagram – that could save the company billions of dollars annually.Meta has relied on a hybrid system of automated filters and thousands of human reviewers to police its platforms for harmful content, and handle user appeals. However, this balance has shifted as the report said that Meta has already replaced roughly half of all human review requests with LLMs this year. The company reportedly aims to push that number even higher by the end of the year, cutting human intervention by up to 90% for specific categories of content.While insiders point to massive financial savings, Meta publicly maintains that the overhaul is about accuracy rather than cutting costs.The company claims that initial testing since March shows LLMs actually outperform people, making 13% fewer mistakes than human reviewers while successfully catching 10% more policy violations.“The point of this work is to improve our enforcement efforts, and we’re deploying these more advanced AI systems once we’re sure they’re consistently performing better than our current methods of content enforcement,” Meta said in a statement.The rollout has sparked concern among some Meta employees, who warn that the technology is being deployed without adequate oversight, the report said. Unlike traditional AI, which relies on rigid keywords, generative LLMs are supposed to understand context.Yet, insiders are reported to say the models still routinely struggle with nuance, sarcasm and evolving internet slang. The report also said that one Meta insider revealed that the AI keeps making frustrating errors, mistakenly deleting harmless posts or “shadow-banning” users, a practice where an account's content is secretly blocked from appearing in public feeds.Furthermore, the sudden shift is already triggering heavy layoffs among external contracting firms, with Meta expected to cancel multiple upcoming contracts with third-party moderation groups, the report said.
Article Summary: Meta is reportedly accelerating plans to replace thousands of human content moderators with generative artificial intelligence (AI) The transition comes as part of a broader, company-wide cost-cutting drive as the Mark Zuckerberg-led company is spending billions of dollars on advanced AI infrastructure.

Article Title: YouTube Shorts now play at double speed with one tap
Article Text:
YouTube Shorts got a speed button you'll actually use

What else is new on Shorts?

YouTube Shorts now plays at double speed. Of the four changes YouTube began rolling out on June 25, this is the one viewers actually asked for—the company says speed ranked among the most requested Shorts features.It works the way long-form viewers already expect. Tap and hold on a Short, then drag down to lock 2x for the rest of the clip. The payoff shows up most on Shorts that creep toward the three-minute mark, where you can blow past the slow bits or jump to the part that matters. People have done this with podcasts and tutorials for years. Now the habit reaches short-form too.The rest are quieter tweaks. Clear Screen mode strips away every overlay for an unobstructed view, a trick TikTok pulled first. Muting takes fewer taps now: pause the video, then hit the mute icon. The thorniest change is the missing dislike button, with YouTube nudging viewers toward "Not Interested" and "Don't recommend this channel," and turning the thumbs-up into a heart.Most of this already lives on Instagram Reels, so YouTube is playing catch-up more than setting the pace. Then again, Shorts pulled 200 billion daily views last year, so it can afford to follow. The rollout is gradual, which means 2x might not hit your feed for a while.
Article Summary: YouTube Shorts now plays at double speed. Of the four changes YouTube began rolling out on June 25, this is the one viewers actually asked for. The rollout is gradual, which means 2x might not hit your feed for a while.

Article Title: Italy launches probe into Microsoft over automatic upgrade to costlier AI subscriptions
Article Text:
Representative Image

Microsoft says it will cooperate



Italy’s antitrust watchdog has announced an official investigation into tech giant Microsoft over alleged unfair and aggressive commercial practices regarding the price increases for its popular “Microsoft 365” subscription service. According to a report by news agency Reuters, the Italian Competition Authority stated that the software company failed to properly inform everyday consumers that it was baking its new artificial intelligence (AI) tools, “Copilot” and “Designer,” into the standard Microsoft 365 package, and passing the bill onto the users.The regulatory watchdog says that Microsoft automatically migrated existing customers into a more expensive subscription tier that included the new AI features. If users did not want the upgrade or the higher fee, they had to dig through their settings to opt out, it added, as per Reuters.The regulator criticised this approach, stating that Microsoft provided “insufficient information” for consumers to make a decision on whether to renew their contracts or accept the new charges.By using this tactics of pushing users onto a pricier plan by default, the watchdog warned that Microsoft's tactics could legally be classified as ‘aggressive’ because they unfairly restricted a consumer’s freedom of choice.The cloud and software giant relies heavily on its subscription models for global revenue, and integrating AI into its core office products has been a centerpiece of its business strategy. However, European regulators are increasingly cracking down on tech companies that use “dark patterns”.Microsoft has signaled it will work alongside local officials to resolve the dispute.“Microsoft is committed to complying with Italian consumer law and will cooperate with the Italian Competition Authority in its preliminary investigation,” a Microsoft company spokesperson was quoted as saying.
Article Summary: Italy's antitrust watchdog has announced an official investigation into tech giant Microsoft over alleged unfair and aggressive commercial practices. The software company failed to properly inform everyday consumers that it was baking its new artificial intelligence (AI) tools into the standard Microsoft 365 package. Microsoft has signaled it will work alongside local officials to resolve the dispute.

Article Title: EU stands firm on digital tax, tells US President Donald Trump: ‘We have the …’
Article Text:
What is digital services tax

Poll Vote & Share your view How do you feel about the potential for a trade war between the US and the EU over digital services taxes? Concerned, it's not good for the economy. Supportive, it's necessary for fair taxation. Indifferent, I don't think it will impact me. 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

The European Union (EU) has pushed back against threats from US President Donald Trump, declaring that European nations hold a sovereign right to regulate and tax economic activity within their own borders. The European Commission issued the statement following a series of warnings from the Trump administration, which has threatened aggressive economic retaliation against European countries that impose digital services taxes on American tech giants like Apple, Google and Meta.A spokesperson for the European Commission clarified that the tax frameworks are built to apply to corporate size and revenue, not nationality. “Any taxes are non-discriminatory by design and apply equally to all large companies, regardless of their ‌origin,” news agency Reuters quoted ⁠a spokesperson as saying. European officials rejected accusations from Washington that the taxes unfairly target American companies.The spokesperson added that the bloc would respond swiftly to ⁠unjustified unilateral measures, but remained open to a global ⁠solution in line with G7 agreements.The digital services tax has been a point of friction between Washington and Brussels for some time now. European governments argue that Silicon Valley giants generate billions of dollars in revenue from European users but pay low corporate taxes by routing their profits through low-tax tax havens like Ireland.Trump vowed to impose a 100% import tariff on any European country that introduces a digital services tax on American technology giants."Numerous European Countries have been discussing the imminent implementation of a Digital Services Tax on American Companies. Some of these Countries are close to actually doing this. Please let this statement serve to represent that any Country that imposes such a Tax will immediately be met with a 100% TARIFF on any and all Goods sent to the United States of America," Trump ⁠said in a social media post. Trump said the new tariff would supersede any trade deals with the US, “whether implemented, signed or not. ”While the EU expressed a strong desire to avoid a trade war, the bloc made it clear that it will not be bullied into dropping the policies.
Article Summary: The European Union (EU) has pushed back against threats from US President Donald Trump. European officials rejected accusations from Washington that the taxes unfairly target American companies. Trump vowed to impose a 100% import tariff on any European country that introduces a digital services tax on American technology giants.

Article Title: Envent Music Lab launched to promote India’s new music talent: All detail
Article Text:
Envent Music Lab

What Envent Music Lab offers

Rohini Ahluwalia, COO of Envent The House Brands

“India has a lot of talent that is yet to be discovered. Envent Music Lab is our effort to give artists a platform, visibility, and support.

We want to create opportunities where passion meets the audience and talent gets the recognition it deserves.”

"🎤 The stage is set. The spotlight is waiting. 🙏 Welcome to Envent Music Lab — where raw talent meets real opportunities. Get ready to showcase your passion, connect with audiences, and take your music to the next level. 🚀 Your sound. Your story. Your moment."

Envent has launched, a new platform aimed at discovering and supporting emerging music talent across India. The initiative is open to singers, musicians, DJs, dancers and other performers, giving them a space to showcase their work, build an audience and participate in live and digital events.The company said creators will be able to upload short-form videos through the platform. Artists whose content receives significant engagement will receive support from the Envent team while retaining ownership and intellectual property rights in their work.Envent will launch a YouTube channel for this initiative that will feature performances by Music Lab artists. Envent will also give out monthly awards and recognition to performers based on their performances.In addition, Envent said it intends to organise concerts and digital events featuring selected artists from the Music Lab community, providing them with opportunities to perform before larger audiences.Explaining the objective behind the platform,, said,According to the company, Envent Music Lab is now accepting entries from artists looking to showcase their talent and grow their audience. The platform is designed to encourage creators to produce original content while allowing them to retain ownership of their work.Announcing the launch on Instagram, Envent The House Brands posted:With the launch of Envent Music Lab, the company is bringing together talent discovery, digital content, artist promotion, and live performances on a single platform to help emerging performers connect with wider audiences.
Article Summary: Envent has launched, a new platform aimed at discovering and supporting emerging music talent across India. The initiative is open to singers, musicians, DJs, dancers and other performers, giving them a space to showcase their work, build an audience and participate in live and digital events.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Walt Disney: “If you can dream it, you can do it”
Article Text:
Quote of the day by animation pioneer Walt Disney



“If you can dream it, you can do it”

What Walt Disney is really saying



Why the quote still matters



About Walt Disney



There are moments in every life when the path forward feels unclear. The goal exists but we feel iys somewhere in the distance and the distance feels too great, the obstacles too real and the certainty too elusive to take the first step with any confidence. It is in exactly those moments that certain words cut through everything else and land with unusual force. One of the most famous lines by Walt Disney – nine words, no complicated philosophy, no caveats – has been doing that for generations. And the reason it keeps working is not because it is simple. It is because it is true and Disney ’s own life is the evidence.At first reading, the quote sounds like the kind of thing you might find stitched onto a cushion or find it on social media platforms. But when looked at it in true sense, it feels that it carries a more serious claim than it first appears to make. Disney is not saying that dreaming is enough; He is saying that dreaming is the necessary beginning: the thing that has to come first before any of the harder work can follow. Without a clear picture of what you are trying to build, effort has no direction. Courage has nothing to attach itself to. Persistence becomes motion without destination.His own story makes the argument better than any explanation could. It is famous that Disney was rejected by newspapers early in his career for lacking imagination. His first animation studio went bankrupt. Mickey Mouse – the character that would eventually become the foundation of a global entertainment empire – was created after he lost the rights to an earlier character he had built. Disneyland, which is now visited by tens of millions of people every year, was dismissed by financiers and city planners as a fantasy that could not be built and would never work.At every stage, the dream preceded the achievement. And at every stage, the people around him were more focused on the obstacles than on the destination he could already see clearly. That is the core of what this quote is asking. Not blind optimism. Not the belief that wishing for something makes it real. But the discipline to hold a clear vision firmly enough that the obstacles along the way become problems to solve rather than reasons to stop.The world has changed considerably since Disney built his empire. But the experience of having a goal and being unsure whether to pursue it has not changed at all. Most people carry dreams they have not acted on; a business they have imagined starting; a skill they have always meant to develop seriously; a project that keeps appearing in their thoughts without ever making it onto their calendar. The reasons for delay are always reasonable: The timing is not quite right; the resources are not quite there; the outcome is not quite certain enough.Disney’s quote does not address any of those specific obstacles, which is part of why it travels so well across generations and circumstances. It does not promise that the path will be easy or that the dream will be achieved exactly as imagined. It simply makes one clear claim: that the dream has to come first. Every significant thing that exists in the world began as something one person could picture clearly when no one else could see it yet.Walter Elias Disney was born in Chicago in 1901 and grew up in Missouri. His early career involved a series of failures that would have stopped most people before they had properly started. His first animation company folded. His early characters did not find audiences. He was dismissed, underfunded, and underestimated through much of his early career. Mickey Mouse arrived in 1928. Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs, the world's first feature-length animated film, came in 1937 against near-universal industry scepticism. Disneyland opened in 1955. Disney World followed in 1971, after his death in 1966.He won 22 Academy Awards and built an entertainment company that today spans films, theme parks, television networks, and streaming platforms with a presence in virtually every country on earth. He is remembered as a dreamer. But the fuller truth is that he was a dreamer who dared — repeatedly, in the face of repeated failure — and that combination is what the quote is really about.
Article Summary: Walt Disney is remembered as a dreamer who dared — repeatedly, in the face of repeated failure — and that is what the quote is really about. Without a clear picture of what you are trying to build, effort has no direction. Courage has nothing to attach itself to. Persistence becomes motion without destination.

Article Title: Netflix now needs users to link email addresses to individual profiles on shared accounts: Here's what's changing
Article Text:
What changes Netflix is bringing for shared account users



“This sign-in update is a permanent change that started rolling out on June 15, 2026.”

Netflix recently started rolling out a sign-in update that requires users to link a unique email address to individual profiles on shared accounts. This will allow each profile owner to sign in independently and manage certain personal settings without relying on the primary account holder. The company confirmed that the permanent change began rolling out earlier this month and applies to most profiles associated with a Netflix subscription, except those designated as children's profiles. Once an email address is linked, profile owners can receive their own sign-in verification codes, making it easier to access Netflix on new devices and manage login credentials independently.For years, Netflix allowed multiple profiles under a single subscription, but all profiles remained tied to the account owner's email address. Under the new system, each profile can now be associated with its own email address while remaining part of the same household subscription.The update also enables profile owners to manage individual preferences such as subtitle language, audio settings, and display options without affecting other users on the account. Netflix said recommendations will continue to be based on each person's viewing history, with the dedicated email further personalising each profile.Subscribers can add an email address through the "Profile Settings" menu or by using the button included in the notification email sent by Netflix. A Netflix spokesperson told Ars Technica,However, the rollout has prompted mixed reactions from users. Some households have reported confusion after profile users were asked to add an email address to continue using Netflix. Others have raised concerns about privacy, arguing that Netflix does not need additional personal information.Netflix's privacy policy states that it may share users' email addresses with marketing and advertising companies. Users who add an email address may also begin receiving promotional emails from Netflix, although they can unsubscribe from marketing communications.The company also said that if a household member links an email address to a profile, that person will retain access to that profile and be able to view the personal information associated with it. For more information about how we handle your personal data, please see our Privacy Statement.Separately, reports suggesting Netflix would require multifactor authentication for all users starting July 7 have raised concerns online. However, Ars Technica reported that the multifactor authentication update applies only to business partner accounts and will not change how regular Netflix subscribers sign in.This is yet another step after Netflix's recent password-sharing ban, which kicked off in 2023, when the company kept changing its policies regarding sharing accounts at home and offering control over the signing-in process for each profile separately.
Article Summary: Netflix recently started rolling out a sign-in update that requires users to link a unique email address to individual profiles on shared accounts. This will allow each profile owner to sign in independently and manage certain personal settings without relying on the primary account holder. The permanent change began rolling out earlier this month and applies to most profiles associated with a Netflix subscription.

Article Title: US government seizes hundreds of websites streaming FIFA World Cup matches; warn viewers: If you ...
Article Text:
FIFA World Cup 2026 apps to watch matches live on their smartphones

Warning for viewers of FIFA World Cup matches

US led the Operation Offsides in coordination with international law enforcement agencies

Poll Vote & Share your view Do you support the actions against illegal World Cup streaming? Yes No 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

The Department of Justice announced the seizure of nearly 400 sites that were engaged in the unauthorized streaming of matches in the FIFA World Cup Finals in violation of US copyright law. “We have seized hundreds of domains, used to illegally stream World Cup matches for profit, to disrupt the international networks that profit from the global popularity of the World Cup,” said Assistant Attorney General A Tysen Duva of the Justice Department’s Criminal Division. “This operation illustrates the Department’s respect for intellectual property rights and the responsibility of the United States as a host nation to protect the FIFA World Cup from criminals. The Criminal Division will continue to disrupt and, where appropriate, seek to prosecute these sites and the subjects responsible for this criminal activity.”“The unauthorized broadcast of World Cup matches violates intellectual property rights and fuels criminal organizations,” said Director Ivan J Arvelo of the National Intellectual Property Rights Coordination Center. “Through Operation Offsides and strong partnerships with law enforcement and the private sector, we identified and seized hundreds of domains, disrupting those who steal and distribute copyrighted content. Our ongoing efforts ensure that the excitement of the World Cup reaches fans through legitimate, secure channels.”“When you open your network to illegal streaming sites, you’re taking a significant risk,” said Special Agent in Charge Eric Weindorf of Immigration and Customs Enforcement Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) Washington Field Office.“These streamers not only violate copyright laws but also expose viewers to potential threats — including malware attacks and unsecure connections that can compromise personal and financial data. These website takedowns are a critical part of HSI’s strategy to uphold intellectual property laws, ensuring fans have a safe and authentic experience supporting their teams during the World Cup.”According to an affidavit in support of a seizure warrant which was filed in the Eastern District of Virginia, the seized domains were used to illegally offer users copyright-protected content in the form of real-time streams of 2026 World Cup matches as they are being played and first broadcast. In support of the application for seizure authority, HSI special agents confirmed that the seized domains were actively broadcasting World Cup matches without authorization. The seized domains were identified with assistance of FIFA, with further supporting information provided by beIN Media Group, NBC Universal, Motion Picture Association’s Alliance for Creativity and Entertainment (ACE), Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC), and Warner Brothers. FIFA is the international governing body of association football (soccer) and holds the exclusive rights to sanction and stage the FIFA World Cup 2026, which is being hosted in multiple cities in the United States, Canada and Mexico.Law enforcement actions targeting the illegal broadcast of FIFA World Cup matches was coordinated with international partners through the International Computer Hacking and Intellectual Property (ICHIP) Network of US prosecutors. ICHIP-trained officials took part in actions across multiple countries and involved the following:Servers and domains linked to illegal streaming of World Cup games were targeted in Peru and Bulgaria, two known centers of online piracy activity. Additional ICHIP-supported disruptions took place in Croatia, Romania, Poland and Colombia. US authorities provided leads to assist in identifying domains associated with illegal streaming of the World Cup.The US action is part of Operation Offsides, led by the National Intellectual Property Rights Coordination Center, targeting digital piracy domains associated with the World Cup. This operation is being conducted in coordination with HSI Washington, D.C., HSI Attaché offices, as well as private sector and law enforcement partners globally. This initiative focuses on disrupting illegal World Cup streaming and protecting intellectual property rights by identifying and seizing websites facilitating unauthorized broadcasts. Senior Counsel Brian Mund, Assistant Deputy Chief Adrienne Rose, and Acting Deputy Chief Christopher Merriam of the of the Criminal Division’s Computer Crime and Intellectual Property Section and Assistant U.S. Attorney Jacob Mercer for the Eastern District of Virgina are assisting. The Justice Department’s ICHIP prosecutors based in Sao Paul, Brazil and Bucharest, Romania, and the ICHIP for Internet-Based Fraud and Public Health in Washington, D.C. also provided crucial support to this operation.CCIPS investigates and prosecutes cybercrime and intellectual property (IP) crime in coordination with domestic and international law enforcement agencies, often with assistance from the private sector. Since 2020, CCIPS has secured the conviction of over 180 cybercriminals and IP criminals, and court orders for the return of over $350 million in victim funds.
Article Summary: The Department of Justice announced the seizure of nearly 400 sites that were engaged in the unauthorized streaming of matches in the FIFA World Cup Finals in violation of US copyright law. The action is part of Operation Offsides, led by the National Intellectual Property Rights Coordination Center, targeting digital piracy domains associated with the World Cup.

Article Title: Google adds Gemini's conversational search to Play Store
Article Text:
Representative Image

Travel: “Recommend a good map app for international trips.”

Lifestyle: “What app gives a 90s disposable camera aesthetic?”

Hobbies: “Find an app to identify fish while scuba diving.”

Productivity: “Suggest a meal planning app.”

Google has introduced a new Gemini integration with the Play Store, allowing Android users to discover apps, games, and even in-app purchases through conversational search. The feature, first previewed at I/O 2026, is now rolling out gradually, making app discovery more intuitive and interactive. With this update, users can simply ask Gemini prompts such as “Recommend a meditation app” or “Find a productivity app for meal planning”. Gemini responds with Play Store cards showing apps, ratings, and download counts.The integration enables a direct install flow: tapping on an app card slides up the Play Store listing, allowing instant installation without leaving Gemini. Beyond apps, Gemini can also help users search and purchase in-app items, subscriptions, and gift cards. For example, queries like “Buy Spotify gift card” or “Show me in-app purchases for Clash of Clans” are supported. Importantly, Gemini only recommends in-app items for apps already installed on the device, ensuring contextual relevance.Use cases for everyday lifeTo enable the integration, users simply enter a relevant prompt in Gemini, which then guides them to connect the Play Store. The rollout is gradual, meaning not all devices will see the feature immediately.
Article Summary: Google has introduced a new Gemini integration with the Play Store. It allows Android users to discover apps, games, and even in-app purchases through conversational search. The feature, first previewed at I/O 2026, is now rolling out gradually, making app discovery more intuitive and interactive.
